target.title: fix: add auth profile listing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw auth list` is currently unavailable for users trying to inspect saved model auth profiles.
- Why it matters: scripts and troubleshooting flows need a read-only way to confirm configured auth profile ids without reading `auth-profiles.json` directly.
- What changed: added `openclaw auth list` plus `openclaw models auth list`, with text, JSON, and plain output that does not print raw API keys, tokens, or OAuth access tokens.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no auth creation, refresh, login, secret resolution, or provider probing behaviour changed.

Fixes #73305.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #73305
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the model auth CLI had login/setup/order helpers but no read-only list subcommand, and there was no top-level `auth` command registered.
- Missing detection / guardrail: command-registration tests did not cover the expected `auth list` affordance.
- Contributing context (if known): users had to read the auth store JSON directly to inspect saved profiles.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cli/auth-cli.test.ts`, `src/cli/models-cli.test.ts`, `src/commands/models/auth-list.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: both CLI surfaces route to the same read-only list command, and formatted output does not include stored secret values.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is command wiring plus output sanitisation, so command-level and formatter unit tests cover the regression without provider/network setup.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw auth list` and `openclaw models auth list` now print configured model auth profile metadata. `--json` emits structured metadata and `--plain` emits profile ids only.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

```text
Before:
openclaw auth list -> unknown command

After:
openclaw auth list -> read auth profile store -> redacted profile summary
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this adds a read-only CLI command for existing local auth profile metadata. Output intentionally reports credential kind/status only and tests assert raw API keys and OAuth token values are not printed.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Run `openclaw auth list`.
2. Run `openclaw models auth list --json`.

### Expected

- Commands print auth profile metadata without raw secret values.

### Actual

- Current release reports `auth` as unavailable or leaves users without a read-only profile-listing command.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Local command evidence:

- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD` passed.
- `pnpm install` was attempted twice but blocked before tests by Corepack registry timeouts while downloading `pnpm 10.33.0` (`UND_ERR_CONNECT_TIMEOUT`).

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: source-level command wiring for `auth list` and `models auth list`; formatter tests cover redaction of API key, token, and OAuth values.
- Edge cases checked: missing credential marker, OAuth expiry display, cooldown status precedence.
- What you did **not** verify: local Vitest/typecheck/format, because this machine could not download pnpm through Corepack. CI should run the new test files.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: auth metadata output could accidentally reveal secret material.
  - Mitigation: the formatter reports only credential kind and status, and unit coverage asserts raw key/token values are absent.

target.title: fix(agent): route explicit channel targets per recipient
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #41483 by deriving per-recipient agent session keys for `openclaw agent --agent ... --channel ... --to ...` instead of falling back to `agent:<id>:main`.
- Preserves the existing #60614 behavior where `--session-id` takes precedence over the implicit `--agent` main-session fallback.
- Carries forward and credits the focused approach from @pingfanfan in source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/41557, while addressing the review blockers raised there.

## Review notes
- Keep explicit `--session-key` authoritative over `--session-id` lookup.
- Preserve global-session continuity for non-deliverable-channel paths.
- Ensure cross-store session-id scans skip the already-searched primary agent store, not an unrelated default store.

## Validation
- `pnpm check:changed`

ProjectClownfish replacement details:
- Cluster: ghcrawl-157028-autonomous-smoke
- Source PRs: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/41557
- Credit: Credit pingfanfan for the original focused implementation approach in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/41557.; Mention that #60614/#60621 covered the separate --session-id precedence path already fixed on main and should not be regressed.; Address the Codex review findings from #41557: preserve global scope for non-channel derived keys and skip the correct primary agent store when scanning session IDs.
- Validation: pnpm check:changed

- Replace the isStreaming-only steer/interrupt injection gate with lifecycle-aware active/stopped handling for embedded and reply runs.

- Keep the patch narrow and avoid unrelated release, docs, generated baseline, Discord, QA, Docker, and script churn from the source branch.

## Credit
This carries forward the focused fix direction from @jetd1 in #65010 and the earlier steer-mode approach from @scottyallen in #52351. Thanks to both contributors; this PR is a clean replacement branch so attribution is preserved while keeping review scope small.

## Validation

- Credit: Credit @jetd1 and source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/65010 for the isActive+isStopped steer-injection approach.; Credit @scottyallen and source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/52351 for the earlier isActive steer-mode fix direction and tests.; Mention linked reports #48003 and #50880 as duplicate reproductions covered by the replacement fix.

target.title: fix(subagent): resolve runtime model from subagent default instead of parent primary
target.body:
## Summary

Two-part fix for subagent model resolution:

- **Commit 1 — read-side fallback (defense in depth, fbbd3c2d5f):** `resolveSessionModelRef` had no subagent awareness. When `entry.model` was empty (race between the spawn-side write and the gateway-side read at run-start), it fell through to `resolveDefaultModelForAgent`, returning the parent agent's primary model instead of the configured subagent default. Also reorders the `??` chain in `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection` so global `agents.defaults.subagents.model` takes precedence over the spawning agent's own `model` field.
- **Commit 2 — Pi-harness write-side fix (production path, this commit):** `getReplyFromConfig` resolved the run model via `resolveDefaultModel` (which goes through `resolveDefaultModelForAgent` and is not subagent-aware), and post-run persistence in `updateSessionStoreAfterAgentRun` then wrote that model back to `entry.model`, silently flipping every subagent run onto the parent agent's primary. The new `resolveSubagentSessionDefaultModel` helper performs a one-time override after `sessionEntry` is loaded so the Pi runtime boots on the configured subagent default and the post-run write preserves it.

## Failure mode (before)

Config:
```
agents.list[main].model.primary = anthropic/claude-opus-4-7
agents.defaults.subagents.model.primary = openai-codex/gpt-5.5
agents.list[main].subagents.model.primary = openai-codex/gpt-5.5
```

Spawn flow:
1. Parent calls `sessions_spawn(task=..., agentId=main)` with no explicit `model:`.
2. Spawn-side computes the right ref (`gpt-5.5`) and writes `entry.model` / `entry.modelProvider` via `persistInitialChildSessionRuntimeModel`.
3. The Pi runtime starts the run via `getReplyFromConfig`, which resolves `defaultProvider/defaultModel` via `resolveDefaultModelForAgent` — returning Opus (parent primary) — and uses that as the run's `provider/model`.
4. Subagent runs on Opus instead of gpt-5.5.
5. Post-run, `updateSessionStoreAfterAgentRun → setSessionRuntimeModel` writes Opus back to `entry.model`, clobbering the spawn-time gpt-5.5.

Result on disk: `~/.openclaw/agents/<agent>/sessions/sessions.json` shows `model: "claude-opus-4-7"` for entries with `subagentRole: "orchestrator", spawnDepth: 1`, even though `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json` correctly recorded `gpt-5.5`. The read-side fallback in commit 1 only kicks in while `entry.model` is empty — once the post-run write fires, `entry.model` is non-empty and the fallback is skipped.

## Fix

- **Commit 1 — `resolveSessionModelRef`:** when `entry.spawnDepth >= 1` or `entry.subagentRole` is set, fall back to the configured subagent default for this agent before falling through to the agent's primary.
- **Commit 1 — `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection`:** reorder `??` chain to `agentConfig.subagents.model` → `agents.defaults.subagents.model` → `agentConfig.model`.
- **Commit 2 — `resolveSubagentSessionDefaultModel`:** new helper in `directive-handling.defaults.ts` that, given the loaded `sessionEntry`, checks the same subagent shape (`subagentRole` set or `spawnDepth >= 1`) and resolves the configured subagent default. `getReplyFromConfig` invokes it once after `sessionEntry` is loaded and overrides `defaultProvider/defaultModel/provider/model` so the Pi runtime boots and post-run-persists on the right model.

## Tests

- Read-side fallback tests in `src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` cover the runtime subagent fallback (with and without `entry.spawnDepth`).
- Spawn-side chain-order tests cover the case where `agentConfig.subagents.model` is unset, `agents.defaults.subagents.model` is set, and `agentConfig.model` is set — we now return the global default instead of the agent's own primary.
- New tests in `src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.defaults.subagent.test.ts` cover the Pi-harness write-side helper: subagent sessions resolve to the configured subagent default; non-subagent sessions return null so the parent's primary is preserved.
- Existing get-reply test mocks updated to expose the new export.

## Scope

Commit 1: `src/gateway/session-utils.ts`, `src/agents/model-selection.ts`, and their tests.
Commit 2: `src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.defaults.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts`, and one new test plus two existing test-mock updates.
No drive-by changes.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Spawned subagent runs persisted the parent agent's primary model (e.g. `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`) into `~/.openclaw/agents/<agent>/sessions/sessions.json` even though the configured subagent default was `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` and the spawn-side resolver had already written the correct ref. Subsequent turns then ran on the wrong model.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw install on macOS, Node 22, single-agent config with `agents.list[main].model.primary = anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, `agents.defaults.subagents.model.primary = openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, and `agents.list[main].subagents.model.primary = openai-codex/gpt-5.5`. Subagent spawned via `sessions_spawn(task=..., agentId=main)` from a parent Telegram chat with no explicit `model:` argument.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `pnpm openclaw doctor` to confirm config parses cleanly.
  2. Start gateway: `pnpm openclaw gateway start`.
  3. From the active chat, ask the agent to spawn a subagent with no model override.
  4. While the subagent is running and again after it completes, inspect `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json` and `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Inspected `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json` directly with `jq` — entries with `subagentRole: "orchestrator"` and `spawnDepth: 1` now persist `model: "gpt-5.5"` and `modelProvider: "openai-codex"` instead of `claude-opus-4-7`. `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json` continues to record `gpt-5.5` as before, and the gateway run trace prints the `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` provider/model pair when the run starts. Before the fix the same inspection printed `model: "claude-opus-4-7"` for those subagent entries even though the runs file recorded `gpt-5.5`.
- **Observed result after fix**: Subagent runs boot on `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` (visible in CLI run output and provider request logs), and the post-run write preserves that model in `sessions.json`, so subsequent turns of the same subagent session keep using `gpt-5.5`. Parent (non-subagent) sessions still resolve to `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7` as expected.
- **What was not tested**: Cross-agent configurations where the agent has no `subagents.model` and only `agents.defaults.subagents.model` is set were exercised only via the new unit tests, not on a live install. None of the alias-resolution paths were exercised against a real OpenRouter alias index live.

### Post-rebase verification (2026-05-12)

Rebase merged `upstream/main` (`e7e3e903bf`) into the branch and addressed the clawsweeper P2 finding on `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts:404-416`. Specifically: a spawned subagent child entry with no own `providerOverride`/`modelOverride` could still inherit the parent session's persisted `/model` override via `resolveStoredModelOverride` (parent-inheritance branch), silently flipping the run back to the parent's model. `resolveStoredModelOverride` now skips the parent-inheritance branch when the child entry is a subagent session (`subagentRole` set or `spawnDepth >= 1`). Direct child overrides and non-subagent (topic/thread) child sessions are unaffected. New regression coverage in `src/auto-reply/reply/stored-model-override.test.ts`.

Commands run:

- `pnpm tsgo` (core typecheck) -> exit 0, no diagnostics
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/stored-model-override.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.defaults.subagent.test.ts src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts` -> 4 vitest shards passed (236 tests)
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply.block-streaming.test.ts` -> passed (1 test)
- `pnpm format:check` on touched files -> all clean after one auto-fix on `stored-model-override.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on touched files -> 0 warnings, 0 errors

target.title: fix(session): persist memory on auto daily/idle rollover
target.body:
## Summary

Auto-rollover (daily or idle stale-detection) takes a different code path than /reset and /new commands, so the bundled session-memory hook never sees a command:reset event and pre-reset transcripts go unwritten. Low-activity agents that get rolled over at 4am without ever compacting or typing a reset command silently lose their daily memory entry.

This PR fires the same internal command:reset hook on stale rollover with a commandSource of 'auto:daily' or 'auto:idle' so existing listeners (including the bundled session-memory handler) persist memory for automatic reset paths too. Guarded against firing on explicit /reset (handled by emitResetCommandHooks) and on fresh sessions.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #8185, #37955
- Closes #11960
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Human verification

Verified that the hook fires and creates the memory file on automatic reset.

target.title: feat(messages): add responseFooter and enrich /usage full
target.body:
## Summary

_AI-assisted PR. Prepared with AI assistance against a repo snapshot; targeted validation only. See Repro + Verification and Human Verification for exact coverage._

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: OpenClaw had a native `messages.responsePrefix`, but no matching native footer surface for the normal reply pipeline. At the same time, built-in `/usage full` did not surface cumulative context usage inline, there was no inherited config default for response-usage footers, and successful block-streaming flows could drop usage/footer output when final body payloads were intentionally suppressed.
- Why it matters: Users want one native, name-agnostic way to surface identity, model, usage, context, and cost metadata directly on every reply without manual prompt hacks, duplicate footer lines, or per-session setup drift.
- What changed: This PR adds `messages.responseFooter`, extends the shared response-template resolver for late-bound usage/context/cost/session values, enriches built-in `/usage full` with model/context/session/cost data, adds inherited `agents.defaults.responseUsage`, composes built-in usage with custom footers into a single footer block, and preserves footer/usage delivery when block streaming suppresses final body payloads.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add a new `/usage context` mode, does not change message-tool outbound sends or other non-reply egress paths, and does not introduce generic free-form interpolation of arbitrary session fields; supported placeholders remain an explicit allowlist.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #40215
- Closes #12578
- Related #24014
- Related #17832
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: `#24014` and `#17832` are currently closed upstream, so they are referenced as related context even though this PR implements the requested behavior in those threads. This PR intentionally does **not** claim `#39913`, because message-tool outbound sends remain out of scope.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Response metadata was split across two incomplete paths: prefix handling existed as an early-bound template surface, while usage data was appended later as a separate footer line after payload assembly. That meant there was no first-class footer stage, no inherited default for usage footers, and no reliable composition point once successful block streaming had already decided to drop final body payloads.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no seam coverage for “block streaming succeeded and final payloads are empty but usage/footer still needs to be delivered,” and no explicit composition rule for combining built-in `/usage` output with a configured custom footer.
- Contributing context (if known): Usage/context/cost/session values are late-bound and in some cases only trustworthy after the reply completes and session state is refreshed. Existing prefix-only handling was not designed for that later lifecycle stage.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-usage-line.footer.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-usage.test.ts`
  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-reply-pipeline.test.ts`
  - `src/config/runtime-overrides.test.ts`
  - `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts`
  - `src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `/usage full` includes model, context window usage, session key, and estimated cost when available
  - `agents.defaults.responseUsage` is inherited by sessions that do not override it
  - explicit `responseUsage: "off"` remains explicit even when a global default exists, while `PATCH responseUsage: null` clears the override and re-enables inheritance
  - static `messages.responseFooter` values fold with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line to avoid duplicate footer lines
  - successful block streaming still returns a trailing footer/usage payload when final body payloads are intentionally suppressed
  - response prefixes do not leak raw late-bound `{cost}` / `{usageLine}` / context placeholders before those values are known
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The risk is in formatting, inheritance, and reply-payload composition. Helper-level tests catch resolver and composition regressions, while reply-runner seam tests catch the payload-loss and block-streaming lifecycle failures without needing live channel E2E runs.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing prefix-template coverage already exercised the shared resolver pattern; this PR extends that surface and adds footer- and usage-specific cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- New optional `messages.responseFooter` config for the normal reply pipeline.
- `messages.responsePrefix` and `messages.responseFooter` now share the same case-insensitive, allowlisted template resolver for placeholders such as `{model}`, `{provider}`, `{identityName}`, `{effort}`, `{input}`, `{output}`, `{total}`, `{cacheRead}`, `{cacheWrite}`, `{context}`, `{contextMax}`, `{contextPercent}`, `{cost}`, `{usageLine}`, and `{session}`.
- Built-in `/usage full` is enriched to include cumulative context usage, model label, session key, and estimated cost when available.
- New inherited config: `agents.defaults.responseUsage` (`off` | `tokens` | `full`). Sessions without an explicit override inherit this default.
- Session-level explicit `responseUsage: "off"` is preserved even when a global default exists. `PATCH responseUsage: null` still clears the explicit override and returns the session to inherited behavior.
- When both `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` are enabled:
  - static footers compose with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware custom footers suppress the separate built-in usage line
  - cost-only custom footers still compose with built-in usage unless `{usageLine}` is explicitly included
- In successful block-streaming flows, usage/footer text can still be emitted as a trailing payload even when the final body payload is intentionally suppressed.
- For streamed prefixes, unresolved late-bound usage/context/cost placeholders are blanked instead of leaking raw `{cost}`-style tokens.
- Added `docs/response-footer.md` documenting config, placeholders, composition rules, and examples.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [optional responsePrefix applied]
  -> [optional /usage line appended late]
  -> [block streaming may suppress final payloads]
  -> [custom footer unavailable natively, or usage/footer can be lost or duplicated]

After:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [early-bound prefix resolved]
  -> [late-bound usage/context/cost/session template context resolved]
  -> [built-in usage line formatted]
  -> [responseFooter composed with or against usage]
  -> [footer block appended to last text payload or emitted as trailing payload]
  -> [reply sent without duplicate footer lines]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node/TypeScript repo snapshot in an offline CAAS container
- Model/provider: mocked reply-runner and gateway paths with synthetic provider/model/usage metadata
- Integration/channel (if any): normal reply pipeline, successful block-streaming reply pipeline, gateway session patch path, and TUI `/usage` toggle path; no live external channel in this container
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "responseUsage": "full"
    },
    "list": [{ "id": "main", "identity": { "name": "Jarvis" } }]
  },
  "messages": {
    "responsePrefix": "*{identityName}:* {cost}",
    "responseFooter": "{usageLine}\n— {identityName}"
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure `agents.defaults.responseUsage` and optionally configure `messages.responseFooter`.
2. Trigger a normal reply, a successful block-streaming reply, and one or more `/usage` mode changes (`off`, `tokens`, `full`).
3. Inspect the final reply payloads and the effective session `responseUsage` state.

### Expected

- Sessions without an explicit override inherit `agents.defaults.responseUsage`.
- `/usage full` includes per-message tokens plus cumulative context, model, session, and estimated cost when available.
- Static custom footers fold with built-in usage into one footer block, while usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line.
- Successful block streaming does not drop footer/usage output just because the final body payload was suppressed.
- Prefixes do not leak raw late-bound placeholders before values are known.

### Actual

- In the covered paths, inherited usage defaults, explicit `off`, and clear-to-inherit (`null`) semantics behave as expected.
- `/usage full` is enriched with context/model/session/cost data in the covered reply paths.
- Footer composition follows the intended static-vs-usage-aware rules, and successful block streaming still returns footer/usage content as a trailing payload.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Representative snippets from the targeted coverage for this branch:

```text
Enriched built-in /usage full:
  Usage: 12k in / 450 out · cache 2.0k cached / 300 new · ctx 45k/200k (23%) · model gpt-5.4 · session `agent:main:webchat:dm:123`
```

```text
Static footer composed with built-in usage:
  ok

  Usage: 12 in / 3 out
  — footer
```

```text
Usage-aware footer suppressing duplicate built-in usage:
  ok

  ↑12 ↓3
```

```text
Successful block streaming with final body suppressed:
  streamed chunk: Hello
  trailing payload returned: Usage: 12 in / 3 out · cache 4 cached / 2 new
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - reviewed the touched config/type/schema/help/label surfaces for `messages.responseFooter` and `agents.defaults.responseUsage`
  - reviewed the reply-runner composition path for normal replies and the success path where block streaming suppresses final body payloads
  - inspected the new resolver/composition tests for footer, usage, inheritance, and prefix late-binding behavior
  - ran `git diff --check`
  - parsed changed TypeScript files successfully with the TypeScript compiler API in the offline container
  - manually exercised targeted helper/runtime behavior for prefix soft-blanking, cost-only footer composition, and enriched full usage-line formatting
- Edge cases checked:
  - explicit `off` versus inherited default
  - `PATCH responseUsage: null` re-enables inheritance
  - static footer versus usage-aware footer versus cost-only footer composition
  - blank or whitespace-only footer templates emit nothing
  - unresolved footer placeholders remain literal, while unresolved late-bound prefix placeholders are blanked
  - successful block streaming can return only a trailing footer payload without prepending a verbose new-session notice
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
  - live Telegram/Discord/WhatsApp channel behavior
  - message-tool outbound send behavior (`#39913` remains intentionally out of scope)
  - screenshot-based verification in a running OpenClaw instance

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No review conversations exist yet because this PR body is being prepared before the PR is opened. Once the PR is live, the author should update these checkboxes based on the actual review state.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk:
  - Enabling both built-in `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` could produce confusing duplicate footer lines.
  - Mitigation: Added explicit composition rules and tests that suppress the separate built-in usage line when the footer already consumes usage-aware placeholders.
- Risk:
  - Late-bound usage/context/cost values could be missing or stale when templates render.
  - Mitigation: The footer path resolves against late-bound reply/session data, prefers refreshed persisted session state when available, and soft-blanks unresolved late-bound prefix placeholders instead of leaking raw tokens.
- Risk:
  - Block-streaming channels may render the usage/footer as a separate trailing message rather than mutating the already-streamed body.
  - Mitigation: The implementation preserves delivery without trying to retroactively rewrite the streamed content, and dedicated reply-runner coverage locks in the suppressed-final-payload success path.
- Risk:
  - Inherited default semantics could confuse users if explicit `off` is not distinguishable from “unset.”
  - Mitigation: Explicit `off` is preserved in session state and TUI patching, while `PATCH responseUsage: null` remains the clear-to-inherit path; both behaviors are covered by tests and docs.

target.title: fix(memory): block symlink escapes in memory_get
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `memory_get` could accept a path that lexically stayed under the workspace or an allowed extra path, but escaped through an intermediate directory symlink.
- Why it matters: an agent using `memory_get` could be tricked into pulling content from outside the workspace or QMD memory boundary, which means normal memory lookups could unexpectedly surface unrelated local files instead of only the notes the user intended memory tools to access.
- What changed: resolve target files and allowed roots to real paths before reading, then reject reads whose canonical path falls outside the allowed root; applied to both builtin memory reads and QMD-backed reads.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no changes to memory search ranking, indexing behavior, prompt logic, or non-memory file access surfaces.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the read path checks only validated the lexical path prefix and direct symlink files, but did not verify that the resolved real path still stayed inside the allowed workspace / extraPath / QMD collection root.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no regression test covered an intermediate directory symlink that escaped the allowed root.
- Contributing context (if known): builtin and QMD read paths each enforced path rules separately, so both needed the same canonical-path containment check.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.read-file.test.ts`
  - `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: reject `memory_get` reads when a path under `memory/`, an allowed extra path, or a QMD collection crosses the boundary through an intermediate directory symlink.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in path resolution and file-read authorization, so direct read-path tests cover it without requiring full indexing or end-to-end setup.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing tests already covered direct symlink files; this PR extends coverage to directory symlink escapes.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`memory_get` now rejects markdown paths that escape the allowed workspace / extra path / QMD collection through intermediate directory symlinks.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
memory_get("memory/linked/secret.md")
-> lexical prefix check passes
-> linked/ resolves outside workspace
-> file is read

After:
memory_get("memory/linked/secret.md")
-> lexical prefix check passes
-> realpath resolves outside allowed root
-> request fails with "path required"
```
## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `Yes`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This narrows the readable file scope for `memory_get` to the canonical allowed roots and blocks previously unintended cross-boundary reads through directory symlinks.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node/Bun repo test runner
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): memory core / QMD
- Relevant config (redacted): default local test config

### Steps

1. Create a markdown file outside the workspace or outside an allowed extra path / QMD collection root.
2. Add an intermediate directory symlink inside `memory/`, an allowed extra path, or a QMD collection that points to that outside directory.
3. Run `memory_get` on the symlinked markdown path.

### Expected

- The read is rejected with `path required`.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the read could succeed because only the lexical path prefix was checked.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - builtin memory reads reject directory symlink escapes
  - extra-path memory reads reject nested directory symlink escapes
  - QMD reads reject directory symlink escapes
- Edge cases checked:
  - direct symlink-file rejection still holds
  - transient file disappearance path still returns empty text correctly after switching reads to canonical paths
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full `pnpm test`
  - full `pnpm check`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: setups that used a directory symlink inside `memory/`, an allowed extra path, or a QMD collection as an implicit way to reach files outside the configured memory roots will now fail on `memory_get`.
  - Mitigation: supported outside-workspace reads should be configured explicitly through `memorySearch.extraPaths` or QMD collections; this change aligns runtime reads with the documented memory-root contract and adds regression coverage for the escaped-symlink case.

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `61e6f29bed` (config-help owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]/[P3]).** Restacked onto #85664's new base `7f9876c2b2`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `661d6838c0` is **config help / type docs** (verified: `git diff 661d6838c0 61e6f29bed` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts` +4 and `src/config/types.gateway.ts` +25 — comment/string lines; every runtime file under `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, `schema.labels.ts` is byte-identical): the `hostFsWrite` `FIELD_HELP` string and the `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` summary + `hostFsWrite` member comment now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces, and the type summary is refreshed so it no longer calls `hostFsWrite` "future work". This directly resolves ClawSweeper **[P2]** (config help omits the owner gate) and **[P3]** (stale `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` summary). Because no runtime code changed, the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container: `schema.help.quality.test.ts` (23/23) and `pnpm check:docs` both green.

---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `661d6838c0` (docs owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]).** Restacked onto #85664's docs-updated read base `ca46eb9946`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `afe36ccf45` is **docs**: the `read` + `write`/`edit` truth tables in `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` and the `hostFsRead`/`hostFsWrite` bullets in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces — directly resolving ClawSweeper's **[P2]** ("Document the owner gate for file-tool opt-ins"). No source files changed (verified: `git diff afe36ccf45 661d6838c0` touches only those two docs files), so `dist/` is byte-identical and the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. `pnpm check:docs` passes in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container (format clean, markdownlint 0 errors, MDX ok, 0 broken links).

---

Builds on #85664. Narrow extension of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Extends #85664's narrow read-only landing to also expose `write` and `edit` over the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the `resolveGatewayScopedTools` resolver). Same **triple-key owner-gated** shape as #85664's `read`: a surface-specific class opt-in **plus** the per-tool name in `gateway.tools.allow` **plus** `senderIsOwner === true`, all on the `http` surface.

## Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base (head `afe36ccf456f`, base upstream `023ce6e96c38`)

This branch was rebuilt on top of #85664's current head `5ca72af1ba` (itself rebased onto current upstream `023ce6e96c38`) and the write/edit extension re-applied with the **same owner-gate**. This directly resolves the prior ClawSweeper [P1].

**Bot [P1] (resolved):** upstream commit `02182d5a3031` ("refactor: remove sender owner tool gating") deleted the `ownerOnly` enforcement helper, so the earlier `ownerOnly: true` metadata tag this PR added became dead code — a trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller could have reached an allowlisted mutating tool with no owner check. **Fix:** this PR no longer relies on that tag. `write`/`edit` are materialized into the direct-invoke candidate set **only when the resolved sender is owner** (`senderIsOwner === true`, evaluated at resolve time in `tool-resolution.ts`), exactly mirroring #85664's read gate. A trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller is now refused even with both config keys set (proven below, cases 2 & 4).

## Triple-key gating

To reach a write-class tool on the direct-invoke surface, ALL must hold:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsWrite: true` | Class-level opt-in for the whole write family. | `false` |
| `gateway.tools.allow: [...]` includes the tool name | Per-tool enablement. | not present |
| `senderIsOwner === true` | Caller resolves to owner/admin (trusted-proxy `operator.admin`, or shared-secret bearer). | — |

Truth table (tool name `T` ∈ `{write, edit}`, surface = `http`):

| `allow` ∋ `T` | `hostFsWrite` | sender owner | `T` reachable |
|---|---|---|---|
| no  | no  | —   | ❌ |
| yes | no  | yes | ❌ (class opt-in off; built-in not materialized; `dangerous_allow` audit fires) |
| no  | yes | yes | ❌ (not in allow) |
| yes | yes | **no**  | ❌ (**owner gate** — refused) |
| yes | yes | yes | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Adds:** `write`, `edit` to direct-invoke (behind `hostFsWrite: true` + allow + owner).
- **Inherits from #85664:** `read` via `hostFsRead: true` + allow + owner.
- **`apply_patch`:** present in `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` for defense-in-depth, but the coding-tools factory produces no `apply_patch` entry, so it is never materialized (proven below, case 7).
- **NOT exposed:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` — RCE-class; out of scope for this PR.

## Audit

- `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_write_allow` fires only when BOTH `hostFsWrite: true` AND a write-class name is in `allow` (mirrors `host_read_allow`); escalates to `critical` on non-loopback bind.
- `dangerous_allow` suppression for `write`/`edit` is **source-aware** — suppressed only when the `hostFsWrite` class opt-in is active; `apply_patch` is never suppressed.

## Files changed

- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — adds `hostFsWrite?: boolean` to `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns`
- `src/config/zod-schema.ts` — `hostFsWrite` schema entry
- `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/schema.labels.ts` — field help + label
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — adds `"write"`, `"edit"` to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — owner-gated write/edit materialization; coding built-ins win plugin name collision when class opt-in active
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `host_write_allow` finding; source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression
- `src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` — write coding-tool collision precedence (owner→built-in, non-owner→plugin, opt-in-off→plugin)
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — `host_write_allow` dual-key + source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression cases (incl. `apply_patch` never suppressed)
- `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` — coding-tool factory plumbing
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md`, `docs/gateway/security/audit-checks.md` — `hostFsWrite` opt-in, triple-key truth tables, `host_write_allow` catalog row

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Extend #85664's owner-gated direct-invoke `read` to also expose `write` and `edit` over the Gateway HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke` surfaces, behind the same triple key (class opt-in `directInvoke.hostFsWrite` + per-tool `gateway.tools.allow` + `senderIsOwner`). Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base so write/edit materialize only for an owner sender — resolving the prior [P1] where upstream `02182d5a3031` made the old `ownerOnly` tag dead code.

**Real environment tested:** Built fresh from head `afe36ccf456f` inside a `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container (full runtime build → `dist/index.js`; SDK → `packages/sdk/dist/index.mjs`). Each case boots a live `openclaw gateway run` on a real loopback socket; owner = trusted-proxy `operator.admin` (and a shared-secret bearer token for the SDK case), non-owner = trusted-proxy `operator.write`. Target file states are re-read from disk after each call to prove real host mutation or its absence.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** `PROOF_HEAD=afe36ccf456f bash .proof-63919.sh` inside the container — boots nine gateway configs and dispatches `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` over HTTP `/tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke`, asserting the HTTP status and re-reading each target file from disk.

**Evidence after fix:** live terminal output captured from the container at head `afe36ccf456f`:

```
=== HEAD: afe36ccf456f ===

### LIVE PROOF (HTTP trusted-proxy surface) ###

--- 1 W-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 24 bytes to /work/.proof63/w1.txt"}]}}
  file=OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f
--- 2 W-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=PRESEED
--- 3 E-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully replaced 1 block(s) in /work/.proof63/e3.txt."}],"details":{"diff":" 1 ALPHA\\n-2 BETA\\n+2 GAMMA-afe36ccf456f", ...}}}
  file=ALPHA / GAMMA-afe36ccf456f
--- 4 E-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: edit"}}
  file=ALPHA / BETA  (unchanged)
--- 5 W-DENIED-no-opt-in (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=false, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 6 W-DENIED-no-allow (allow=[] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 7 APPLYPATCH-denied (allow=[apply_patch] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=apply_patch) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: apply_patch"}}
--- 8 READ-REGRESSION (allow=[read] hostFsRead=true, operator.admin, tool=read) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"READ-BASE-OK-afe36ccf456f\\n"}]}}

### LIVE PROOF (SDK token surface — shared-secret bearer = owner) ###

--- 9 SDK-W-OWNER (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, token bearer, tool=write) ---
  [SDK token] {"ok":true,"toolName":"write","output":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 28 bytes to /work/.proof63/w9.txt"}]},"source":"core"}
  file=SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f

=== PROOF COMPLETE: HEAD=afe36ccf456f ===
```

Independent re-read of persisted file states after the run: `w1.txt`=`OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`, `w2.txt`=`PRESEED` (non-owner write left it untouched), `e3.txt`=`ALPHA/GAMMA-afe36ccf456f`, `e4.txt`=`ALPHA/BETA` (non-owner edit left it untouched), `w5.txt` and `w6.txt` absent (never created), `w9.txt`=`SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`.

**Observed result after fix:** All nine cases behaved as specified — owner writes/edits land and mutate the host file; a non-owner `operator.write` caller is refused `write`/`edit` (404) with the target file left untouched; missing class opt-in or missing allow entry both deny; `apply_patch` is never materialized; and the inherited `read` path still works:

| # | Case | Config | Caller | HTTP/SDK | File state |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | write owner | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 wrote 24 bytes | written |
| 2 | write **non-owner** | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 3 | edit owner | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 replaced 1 block | edited |
| 4 | edit **non-owner** | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 5 | write no opt-in | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=false | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 6 | write no allow | allow=[], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 7 | apply_patch | allow=[apply_patch], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | factory never materializes it |
| 8 | read regression | allow=[read], hostFsRead=true | `operator.admin` | 200 + contents | #85664 base intact |
| 9 | write owner (SDK) | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | token bearer | ok=true wrote 28 bytes | written |

Supplemental (not a substitute for the live proof above), all in the same `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64 container at head `afe36ccf456f`: unit tests — **160 passed** across the gateway test projects (`tools-invoke-http` 42 + `tool-resolution` 5 + `audit-gateway-tools-http` 19, ×3 projects = 7 file-projects); `lint` (oxlint, 0/0), `tsgo:core` (0), `check:test-types` (0), and `plugin-sdk-surface-report` vitest (4/4). **`checks-node-core-fast` / plugin-SDK surface budget:** this PR adds `hostFsWrite?` as a *field* on the existing `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` type — **zero new top-level exports** — so `plugin-sdk-surface-report --check` passes on the `+1` budget already carried by #85664's base commit (`config-types` 416 / public 10285 / public-fn 5164 / deprecated 3238). No further budget change in this PR.

**Not tested:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` (RCE-class; out of scope for this PR and never exposed by the direct-invoke factory).

target.title: [codex] prefer terminal reply fields in CLI JSONL parser
target.body:
﻿﻿## Summary

- Problem: the CLI JSONL parser only special-cases Claude `type:"result"` events and otherwise falls back to generic text collection, so OpenAI/Codex-style fallback runs can lose terminal reply semantics.
- Why it matters: in channel-visible fallback paths, commentary/intermediate text can be surfaced instead of the actual final answer even when the JSONL stream already contains `phase:"final_answer"` and `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
- What changed: `parseCliJsonl()` now prefers `task_complete.last_agent_message`, then assistant messages tagged `phase:"final_answer"`, and skips `commentary`-phase generic item text.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Claude CLI handling is unchanged, no dispatch/channel code changed, and no provider failover logic changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #59918
- Related #25592
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `parseCliJsonl()` was written around Claude `type:"result"` lines plus a generic `parsed.item.text` fallback, but it did not understand newer OpenAI/Codex-style JSONL terminal fields.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no parser preference for `task_complete.last_agent_message` or `phase:"final_answer"`, and no guard against treating `commentary` item text as the outbound reply.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the current behavior is discussed in #59918 as a narrower parser-path issue adjacent to the broader channel-leak family in #25592.
- Why this regressed now: unknown as a specific regression point; this appears more like a compatibility gap exposed by fallback runs that emit richer JSONL event shapes.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: this fix does not depend on channel dispatching, failover selection, or Claude stream-json parsing.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when JSONL contains commentary, `final_answer`, and `task_complete`, the parser returns the terminal answer; when only `final_answer` is present, it wins over commentary text.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives entirely inside the JSONL parsing function and can be reproduced with synthetic line fixtures without needing a live provider or channel.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing Claude result tests in `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` cover the Claude-specific path and remain unchanged.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenAI/Codex-style CLI fallback runs now prefer terminal reply fields instead of surfacing commentary when both are present in JSONL.
- Commentary-only generic item text is no longer treated as the final reply in this parser path.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
CLI JSONL -> generic text collection -> commentary can win -> user sees wrong reply

After:
CLI JSONL -> task_complete / final_answer preferred -> terminal answer wins -> user sees final reply
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (local contributor checkout)
- Runtime/container: Node.js + pnpm
- Model/provider: parser coverage for OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output; Claude path rechecked via existing tests
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A for unit verification
- Relevant config (redacted): CLI backend `output: "jsonl"`

### Steps

1. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary events plus a terminal `task_complete.last_agent_message`.
2. Feed `parseCliJsonl()` a JSONL stream containing commentary plus assistant `phase:"final_answer"` content.
3. Run the targeted parser tests.

### Expected

- Terminal answer fields win over commentary.
- Claude JSONL result handling still passes unchanged.

### Actual

- Verified with targeted tests after the patch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/cli-output.test.ts src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked: existing Claude stream-json tests still pass; `task_complete` outranks `final_answer`; `final_answer` outranks commentary; commentary `item.text` is ignored.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not run a live WhatsApp/Telegram fallback end-to-end against a real CLI provider from this branch.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI/Codex-style CLI JSONL output is now parsed so terminal reply records win over commentary or intermediate message text in both the full-buffer parser and streaming parser.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout at PR head `8b90301ef3`; installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5`; Node `v24.13.1`; OpenClaw parser entry points imported directly from `src/agents/cli-output.ts`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK" 1> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.jsonl 2> .artifacts/real-proof-59920/codex-ignore-rules.stderr.txt

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports parseCliJsonl() and createCliJsonlStreamingParser() from ./src/agents/cli-output.ts
feeds the saved Codex CLI JSONL transcript through both parser paths

node --import tsx --input-type=module
imports the same parser entry points and replays redacted Codex/OpenAI terminal-field JSONL containing commentary, final_answer, and task_complete records
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ codex exec --json --ephemeral --ignore-rules --sandbox read-only --skip-git-repo-check "Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"
{"type":"thread.started","thread_id":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95"}
{"type":"turn.started"}
{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_0","type":"agent_message","text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK"}}
{"type":"turn.completed","usage":{"input_tokens":22858,"cached_input_tokens":2432,"output_tokens":129,"reasoning_output_tokens":116}}

real_codex_full_buffer_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
real_codex_streaming_output=
{"text":"FINAL_PROOF_IGNORE_RULES_OK","sessionId":"019e228b-0ab8-7ea1-9675-ea6149bafe95","usage":{"input":22858,"output":129,"cacheRead":2432}}
```

```text
terminal_field_jsonl=
{"type":"response_start","thread_id":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
{"item":{"type":"message","phase":"commentary","text":"COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN"}}
{"type":"response_item","payload":{"type":"message","role":"assistant","phase":"final_answer","content":[{"type":"output_text","text":"FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN"}]}}
{"type":"event_msg","payload":{"type":"task_complete","last_agent_message":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS"}}
full_buffer_output=
{"text":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS","sessionId":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
streaming_output=
{"text":"TASK_COMPLETE_WINS","sessionId":"thread-real-proof-redacted"}
```

Observed result after fix: The installed Codex CLI produced JSONL on this machine and both OpenClaw parser paths extracted the final agent message from that transcript. For the bot-cited terminal-field shape, both parser paths returned `TASK_COMPLETE_WINS`; `COMMENTARY_SHOULD_NOT_WIN` and the lower-priority `FINAL_ANSWER_SHOULD_BE_OVERRIDDEN` were not surfaced.

What was not tested: A live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery route was not exercised for this parser-only PR.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a non-Claude JSONL backend could intentionally rely on commentary-phase `item.text` as the only final output.
  - Mitigation: the parser now only skips explicit `commentary` phase text and prefers explicit terminal fields; generic non-commentary `item.text` fallback remains intact.

target.title: feat: cute GTK-native Linux App (#75)
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: - OpenClaw did not have an in-repo native Linux companion app, so Linux users were missing a first-class desktop surface for gateway lifecycle, diagnostics, tray/status behavior, onboarding, and key operational management flows. - The existing Linux direction in #75 called for a GTK-native companion rather than treating Linux as only a wrapper or dashboard handoff. - Why it matters: - Linux is a real target platform in the issue, and the lack of a native companion left users without parity on core desktop workflows such as service state visibility, tray/status integration, local diagnostics, and direct management of gateway-backed sections. - Without a native app, Linux-specific concerns such as systemd user-service integration, Ubuntu GNOME tray behavior, local-vs-remote resource access, and distro-native dependency flow were not handled in a coherent product surface. - What changed: - Added a new native Linux companion app under `apps/linux` using C, GTK4, and Libadwaita, plus a private GTK3 Ayatana/AppIndicator tray helper for Ubuntu GNOME tray/status integration. - Added a native gateway client stack for Linux: config loading, HTTP health probing, authenticated WebSocket connection, typed RPC mutation helpers, gateway data adapters, and controller-based UI sections. - Added native operational management surfaces for Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances, including config-safe editing, locality-aware transcript/log opening, pairing actions, and richer management flows previously absent on Linux. - Added Linux runtime/readiness modeling, onboarding, diagnostics, notifications, systemd integration, and focused automated tests covering parser behavior, mutation request shapes, RPC lifecycle handling, config rebuild safety, runtime/readiness logic, and helper behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): - This PR does not add distro packaging artifacts such as `.deb`, `.rpm`, or Snap packages. - This PR does not attempt to solve the Windows side. - This PR does not claim full macOS parity across every desktop capability; it establishes the native Linux companion foundation and a substantial in-app management surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #75 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - N/A - Scenario the test should lock in: - N/A - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - N/A - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - N/A - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). target.title: Replace SHA-1 with SHA-256 for config fingerprinting
target.body:
## Summary

- Replace `crypto.createHash("sha1")` with `crypto.createHash("sha256")` in two files:
  - `src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts` - MCP server catalog fingerprinting
  - `src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts` - temp media file content hashing in tests

## Motivation

SHA-1 is used here for MCP server catalog fingerprinting and test file caching. The rest of the codebase consistently uses SHA-256 (e.g., `safeEqualSecret` in `src/security/secret-equal.ts`). This change aligns these two remaining SHA-1 call sites with the project's standard hash algorithm.

No functional impact - these hashes are used for cache invalidation and content-addressing, not integrity verification. SHA-256 is a drop-in replacement.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: The remaining bundle MCP fingerprint and media temp-file hash call sites no longer use SHA-1.
- **Real environment tested**: PolyU Linux workspace, OpenClaw checkout refreshed from current `origin/main`, branch `codex-refresh-59695`, inspected with the real `git` and `rg` CLIs against the patched repository.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
git diff -- src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts
rg -n 'createHash\("sha1"\)' src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts || true
rg -n 'createHash\("sha256"\)' src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
The SHA-1 search returned no output.
src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts:68:  return crypto.createHash("sha256").update(JSON.stringify(normalizedServers)).digest("hex");
src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts:233:  const digest = crypto.createHash("sha256").update(content).digest("hex");
```

- **Observed result after fix**: Both patched files now resolve to SHA-256 call sites, and the previous `createHash("sha1")` pattern is absent from those files.
- **What was not tested**: Full project test suite was not run locally because this checkout has no installed `node_modules`; CI should run the complete project checks.

target.title: fix(telegram): clarify inbound document fallback reason
target.body:
## Summary

* Problem: Inbound Telegram document download failures could degrade into a placeholder-only body like `<media:document>` with no explanation.
* Why it matters: This makes attachment failures ambiguous and harder for the agent to interpret, especially for Bot API size-limit cases.
* What changed: Preserve `<media:document>` for failed inbound document downloads and append an explicit reason line for `file too large` vs `download failed`.
* What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add support for downloading Telegram files over the Bot API size limit and does not change proxy, timeout, SSRF, or unrelated media behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

* [x] Bug fix
* [ ] Feature
* [ ] Refactor required for the fix
* [ ] Docs
* [ ] Security hardening
* [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

* [ ] Gateway / orchestration
* [ ] Skills / tool execution
* [ ] Auth / tokens
* [ ] Memory / storage
* [x] Integrations
* [ ] API / contracts
* [ ] UI / DX
* [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

* Related #55917
* [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

* Root cause: Failed inbound Telegram document downloads could fall back to placeholder-only output without surfacing why the attachment was unavailable.
* Missing detection / guardrail: Existing coverage did not lock in the final rendered body behavior for failed inbound document downloads.
* Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): Scoped to the Telegram inbound document fallback path discussed in #55917.
* Why this regressed now: The fallback path preserved message delivery, but not enough user-visible context to explain attachment unavailability.
* If unknown, what was ruled out: Ruled out broader Telegram transport, proxy, timeout, SSRF, and Bot API size-limit behavior changes as part of this fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

* Coverage level that should have caught this:

  * [x] Unit test
  * [x] Seam / integration test
  * [ ] End-to-end test
  * [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
* Target test or file:

  * `extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.resolve-media-retry.test.ts`
  * `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.media-fallback.test.ts`
* Scenario the test should lock in:

  * successful document download path remains unchanged
  * failed document download returns an explicit fallback reason
  * file-too-large and generic download failure are distinguished
  * final rendered body keeps `<media:document>` and includes the unavailable reason
  * text + failed document preserves user text, placeholder, and reason
* Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug spans both resolver behavior and final body rendering, so focused resolver coverage plus one final body-format test is the smallest reliable guardrail.
* Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing retry coverage covered related resolver behavior, but not final body rendering for failed inbound document downloads.
* If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

* Failed inbound Telegram document downloads no longer degrade into a silent placeholder-only body.
* Failed inbound documents still preserve `<media:document>`.
* The final message body now includes one explicit reason line:

  * `Telegram attachment unavailable: file too large for Bot API download`
  * `Telegram attachment unavailable: download failed`
* Successful inbound document downloads remain unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[inbound Telegram document download fails] -> [body becomes <media:document>] -> [failure reason is unclear]

After:
[inbound Telegram document download fails]
  -> [keep <media:document>]
  -> [append explicit unavailable reason]
  -> [agent sees both document placeholder and failure reason]
```

## Security Impact (required)

* New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
* Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
* New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
* Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
* Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
* If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

* OS: Windows 10
* Runtime/container: local dev environment
* Model/provider: N/A
* Integration/channel (if any): Telegram extension logic
* Relevant config (redacted): standard local repo/test setup

### Steps

1. Receive an inbound Telegram document message.
2. Hit a document download failure in the existing fallback path.
3. Build the final message body for agent ingestion.

### Expected

* The final body keeps `<media:document>`.
* The final body includes an explicit unavailable reason.
* Successful document downloads remain unchanged.

### Actual

* Before this fix, failed inbound Telegram document downloads could degrade into a placeholder-only body such as `<media:document>` with no explicit reason.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

* [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
* [ ] Trace/log snippets
* [ ] Screenshot/recording
* [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

* Verified scenarios:

  * Reviewed the final reduced patch and confirmed it stays scoped to Telegram inbound document fallback messaging.
  * Ran focused local tests for resolver behavior and final body rendering.
* Edge cases checked:

  * successful document download path remains unchanged
  * file-too-large fallback reason
  * generic download-failed fallback reason
  * text + failed document preserves user text, placeholder, and reason
* What you did **not** verify:

  * live end-to-end Telegram bot reproduction

## Review Conversations

* [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
* [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

* Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
* Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
* Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
* If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

* Risk: The unavailable-reason plumbing now flows through Telegram message-context handling.

  * Mitigation: The patch is scoped to failed inbound document handling and covered by focused resolver/body tests.
* Risk: Final body formatting could accidentally drop user text or the placeholder.

  * Mitigation: Focused rendered-body coverage locks in both document-only and text + failed document output.
